text: The surgeon begins the procedure by performing sharp dissection to remove adhesions, preparing the surgical field for the rectopexy.
The surgeon begins the procedure by performing sharp dissection of adhesions, carefully separating the adhered tissues.
The surgeon observes the thickened peritoneal reflection, indicative of chronic changes associated with the patient's rectal prolapse.
The surgeon observes a thickened peritoneal reflection, indicative of chronic changes associated with the rectal prolapse.
The surgeon performs dissection on the posterior aspect of the rectum, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs dissection on the posterior aspect of the rectum, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral side of the rectum, carefully separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral side of the rectum, progressing through the thickened peritoneal reflection.
The surgeon opens the peritoneal reflection on the anterior side, revealing underlying structures as part of the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon opens the peritoneal reflection on the anterior side of the rectum, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon continues the dissection through the pelvis, progressing until the levator muscles become visible.

The surgeon places a rectangular-shaped mesh on the anterior surface of the rectum, securing it in position as part of the rectopexy procedure.
A rectangular-shaped mesh is positioned on the anterior surface of the rectum, covering the area exposed by the dissection.
The surgeon fixes the rectangular mesh to the ventral wall of the rectum using separate sutures, securing it in place.
The rectangular mesh is fixed to the ventral wall of the rectum using separate sutures, securing it in place.
The surgeon secures the rectangular mesh to the sacral promontory using spiral tackers, ensuring stable fixation of the mesh in the pelvic region.
The surgeon secures the rectangular mesh to the promontorium using spiral tackers, ensuring stable fixation on the anterior surface of the rectum.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum to prevent intra-abdominal organs from coming into direct contact with the mesh placed on the anterior surface of the rectum.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum to prevent direct contact between intra-abdominal organs and the mesh.
